data.title: Yum! Brands: Growth And Liquidity Are Tasty, But Overvaluation Spoils Upside Potential
data.description:
Yum! Brands, Inc. (NYSE:YUM) closed the first-half with sustained strength despite the stubborn inflation and stiff competition. Its solid revenue growth and stable margins remain one of its main attractions. This is supported by its strategic business model and robust Balance Sheet, promising sustainability and dividend longevity. However, entering at this current price remains challenging. In addition, its bearish signals are still evident today.

YUM: Tasty Sales Growth, But Spoilt Margins

Yum! Brands, Inc. just released its Q2 2025 results earlier today, and we can say that it’s still doing fine despite the headwinds it faces. Its operating revenue amounted to $1.93B, up by 9.7% YoY from $1.76B. All its revenue components rose, showing a stronger performance in both restaurant and franchising segments, mainly from KFC and Taco Bell.

KFC remains the core revenue growth driver as it comprises over 50% of the total number of YUM-related restaurants. Its system sales increased by 6.0% YoY to $8.72B, faster than the increase in its restaurant count. In fact, KFC generated average sales of $269,424 ($8.72B/32,369) per restaurant versus $268,044 ($8.22B/30,689). This shows that its continued expansion remains fruitful. As a result, YUM derived more franchise fees from KFC franchisees.

The same goes for Taco Bell as its system sales rose by 6.4% YoY to $4.28B. This is the highest sales growth among its restaurants. Each Taco Bell restaurant generated average sales of $488,236, a 4.1% YoY increase from $469,002. With that, we can say that Taco Bell was the most productive restaurant in the group. It’s not surprising that franchise fees also rose to $248M YoY.

However, Pizza Hut and Habit Burger are still having a hard time coping with the challenging market. Somehow, this was expected, especially for Pizza Hut, considering the fierce pizza competition with Domino’s (DPZ) and Papa John’s (PZZA). DPZ dominates the pizza market, particularly the US market. Meanwhile, PZZA is still smaller but increasing in global presence. Pizza Hut appears to have a shrinking presence in the US market. Aside from that, its restaurant count decreased by -96. Even if it reduced its restaurants, its average sales per restaurant weakened from $158,074 to $157,626 YoY. Despite its positive growth adjusted to forex, we can’t deny that Pizza Hut still struggles to regain its footing.

In terms of revenue growth, it is also much better than in Q2 2024 at only 4.5% YoY. Yet, it is important to note that revenue growth appears to be weakening again from Q4 2024. This may have been influenced by various factors, such as inflation. It remains stubborn considering its rebound to 2.4-2.7% coupled with the rate cut pause. This still signals uncertainty, which affects discretionary spending. After all, positive inflation means the general price level is still increasing.

We can also see its impact on operating costs and expenses as the combined amount rose by 13.4% YoY. This completely offset revenue growth. Aside from that, its operating leverage rose from 42.6% to 45.5%, which means that YUM is more cost-sensitive today. As a result, its operating margin decreased from 34.4% to 32.2% YoY.

Why YUM Is Still Strong

Despite the weaker performance of Pizza Hut and higher operating leverage, YUM remains a high-margin business, which is crucial in the current macroeconomic environment. Having a 30% operating margin means a lot to cover its whole operations even without resorting to borrowing. One important aspect to consider is the fact that a substantial portion of its operations is dedicated to franchising, which is less capital-intensive and more resilient to inflation. So, it’s not surprising that it maintains a robust cash flow. In fact, its operating cash flow is enough to cover all investing and financing expenditures, excluding dividend payouts and share repurchases. This means that YUM remains very liquid and sustainable. Aside from that, it generated an FCF of $708M during the first half. This means that YUM converted 19.0% of its revenue into free cash using the FCF/Sales Ratio. This is higher than in the H1 2024 with 17.9%. More interestingly, its actual FCF in Q2 of $374M is also better than in my previous coverage last Q1 at only $313M. Hence, its current operations have higher cash returns both on a YoY and QoQ basis, making it more fruitful today.

YUM complements it with its prudent debt management. It still relies on it at some level, but it continues to repay maturing debt on time. As a result, the total amount remains relatively stable. It’s still high, but YUM will cover it. In fact, its Net Debt/EBITDA keeps decreasing. It’s now 3.8x versus my first and second coverage at 4.1x and 3.9x, respectively. This means that YUM generates more cash and earnings while keeping borrowings stable.

Valuation

To assess it, I updated my P/E Ratio considering the high sensitivity of the stock price to the EPS. Currently, YUM trades at 28.74x its EPS, which is more expensive than the five-year average of only 26.01x. This is also the second-highest P/E since 2020. If we use the average to derive its fair value, the TP will be $132.11. This is quite higher than in my previous coverage at $131.87. Yet, its overvaluation remains evident.

I also made an update on its DCF Model. I increased its Perpetual Growth Rate to 2.0% because I think it’s now reasonable for its recent performance. It’s also more consistent with the US inflation. Meanwhile, I estimated WACC 7.17% using the capital asset pricing model. Doing this is quite straightforward since most of the numbers can be gathered. For instance, the market value of equity is its market capitalization. For the market value of debt, I simply copied its most recent debt and the interest expense as the cost of debt. I also set its risk premium at 5.0% to compensate for the risk to be taken in YUM instead of just enjoying the 10-year treasury yield. For its Beta, I simply computed and compared the five-year daily price change of YUM relative to the S&P 500 (SPX) using the SLOPE function on MS Excel. To be sure, you can just copy the Beta reported on reliable websites like Yahoo Finance, which is 0.73. A Beta lower than 1.00 means that YUM is less volatile than the S&P 500. To be more conservative, I just used 0.76 from my computation.

In the DCF Model, I only updated the value of cash, debt, minority interest, and the number of shares. In the end, I came up with $132.08 as my TP. This is much lower than $172.76 in my previous model. I think my new TP is more precise since this is close to my price valuation using P/E.

Technicals

YUM has early bearish signals, although more indications may be needed to verify this. As we can see, the price has been generally decreasing in the past four weeks. Now, the price has already crossed below the 50-day and 200-day SMAs. In addition, the 50-day SMA has flattened, which shows a potential shift in market sentiments. To confirm this, the price must break below its first key support of $138.42. If not, the downtrend will not hold long. The 50-day SMA must also cross below the 200-day SMA. Considering the current price and SMA pattern, I advise investors to stay on the sidelines.

Meanwhile, MACD reflects its weak momentum. The MACD line and histogram are in negative territory, which shows that more bears are moving in the market than bulls. It also means that selling volume still exceeds buying volume. Its RSI can explain this because the stock became overbought right before the correction. This proves the recent overpricing. As a result, profit-takers entered and pushed the price down. At this point, there is now enough room for reentering at this price. Yet, it will be better to wait for RSI to hit 30 or become nearly oversold. At our TP of $132, it is possible that the sell-off has already stopped. So, holding rather than buying is a safe choice.

Conclusion

Yum! Brands, Inc. remains resilient with KFC and Taco Bell at the forefront, supported by its strategic business model to stabilize revenue growth and capital requirements. Liquidity remains impressive with its robust operating cash flow coupled with prudent debt management. However, entering can be risky amid its overpricing. Technical risks are also emerging, although more confirmations are still needed. I am reiterating my hold rating on this stock.

data.title: So Bullish It Hurts: My Favorite Stocks For AI's Biggest Bottleneck
data.description:
Introduction

Every now and then, in recent years, we've spent some time discussing investment strategies, pitfalls, and overall risks for investors like you and me.

This includes biases. And this includes the following:

  • Loss aversion: Traders fear losses, which can cause them to avoid investing or to "chicken out" too early, selling investments at the worst moments possible.
  • Herd behavior: This comes in many forms as a lot of investors are quickly influenced by others. This can create selling panic and a fear of missing out during strong bull markets.
  • Disposition effect: People tend to sell winners too early and hold onto losers.

These are quite normal, and I'm sure many of my readers are mentally well equipped to deal with most of these "common issues."

My point is that there's a bias that's extremely tricky to deal with, a bias that tends to get me as well. That's the "failure of imagination," and it's exactly what you think it is.

Essentially, it's a bias based on our life experiences and our inability to imagine things that go beyond our mental models. It applies to many areas, including trading, investing, geopolitics, healthcare, and many others.

If I had to visualize it, it would likely look something like this:

The first space flights are a great example of this:

Former astronaut and Eastern Airlines CEO Frank Borman coined an incredibly apt phrase in 1967. Testifying before a House committee investigating the deaths of three astronauts during a ground test, Borman attributed the disaster to “a failure of imagination.” He said that the engineering team had been unable to envision such an occurrence in the first place, and therefore were completely unprepared to prevent it or deal with it when it actually happened. - Business Insider

Other examples can be the supply chain implosion after the 2020 pandemic, when most experts were simply overwhelmed as we were dealing with unprecedented swings in demand.

My point is that based on our mental models, we tend to have an inability to assess the probability of potential future events or massively underestimate just how big certain things can become, as vague as that may sound.

I'm bringing all of this up because I believe this applies to artificial intelligence, a topic I have spent an increasing amount of time on, writing articles with titles like "The Only 2 Places I'd Put New Dividend Money Right Now."

Despite all the coverage, I believe the major AI trend is the perfect example of "failure of imagination" bias, as it's super hard for us to imagine just how big this technological development will be and what this means for investment opportunities.

That's why I'm writing this article.

  • I'm shedding more light on the magnitude of the AI revolution.
  • As usual, I will present food for thought in the form of a few companies I expect the market is completely underestimating.

AI Could Become Way Bigger Than Expected

I believe tech investor Rihard Jarc said it best when he tweeted the following on July 30, noting that we may be just at the start of the AI revolution:

We are still underestimating the massive demand for AI chips, data centers, and energy. The semi cycle with Nvidia, AMD, TSM, and all the others is still in its early phase, even though that sounds crazy.

Peter Thiel said, "We're in a world where nothing big ever happens, so we have no way of processing it when something pretty big happens."

I especially love that he brought up Peter Thiel, who indirectly mentioned the failure of imagination bias as it's truly hard for us to assess just how big of a deal this is.

To give you an idea of just how seriously the big tech companies are taking this subject. In 2Q25 alone, Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), and Microsoft (MSFT) spent more than $70 billion on capex, most of it for AI applications. That's just three companies and up from $5 billion in 3Q15.

Microsoft spent 23% of its entire revenue over the past four quarters on capex, a multi-decade high. Meanwhile, Amazon is spending 16% of its revenue on capex, a number that meets the ratio during the early 2000s, when it was young and working on exploiting the first e-commerce wave.

Here are some more mind-blowing facts (courtesy of Forbes):

  • The AI market is expected to reach $1.3 trillion by 2030. That's up from $214 million in 2024.
  • AI can potentially boost U.S. GDP by more than 20% during this period.
  • More than 75% of consumers are worried about misinformation from AI. Personally, I think that number should be 100% as I have seen people getting fooled by videos that are still obviously made by AI. Once that changes, it will be impossible to know what we can and cannot trust.
  • Also, 10% of all cars will (likely) be self-driving by 2030.
  • AI is expected to see annual growth rates of 37% from 2023 to 2030.
  • Close to 80% of workers are concerned AI will cause job losses next year. There are suggestions that 400 million workers could be displaced by AI.

Just think about it. For now, AI is mainly used through online systems like ChatGPT as a search engine and research tool. In the years ahead, it will power self-driving cars and humanoids, automate entire processes, create movies, art, series, operate on people, develop personalized medicine, and more.

To give you an idea of the developments we're about to witness, we need to work from a base, which is roughly 55 GW in global data center power usage. This is a Goldman Sachs number that consists of 54% demand from cloud computing, traditional workloads (32%), and AI (just 14%).

By 2027, demand is expected to reach 84 GW, with AI accounting for slightly more than a quarter of this demand, a trend that will remain high for many years, if not decades.

This translates to the power demand equal to a nation like Vietnam or Egypt in 2023, an impressive number, but still just the start.

For example, the newest hyperscale data centers like OpenAI Stargate in Texas require 1.2 GW, which is enough to power 1 million homes. Last year, OpenAI already floated the idea of building 5 GW data centers, which will be required to support advanced AI applications as existing data centers are "nothing" compared to what is needed to support advanced AI.

A 5 GW data center requires as much power as New York City, a development that overshadows everything we have dealt with so far.

Then there's the trend of humanoid robots.

Widespread adoption of humanoid robotics in industries like manufacturing, healthcare, and services would drastically increase AI inference workloads.

RAND estimates global AI data center power demand could reach 327 GW by 2030 if exponential compute trends persist, which I expect to happen. Demand would be driven by applications like real-time motor control and natural language processing for millions of humanoids.

The IEA (don't confuse it with the EIA) estimates that in a "lift-off case," demand from aggressive AI adoption, including humanoids, autonomous vehicles, and pervasive AI applications, could push demand to 1,700 TWh (that's roughly 680 GW) by 2035. This could be more than 4.0% of the global power supply.

Essentially, growth is so rapid that it reaches what's physically possible.

This is what I tweeted last month (note that the Musk prediction is not my base-case):

Humanoid robots are a fascinating topic.

- Goldman Sachs estimates demand for 1.4-3.5m units by 2035.

- Each requires 50kW of data center capacity.

- In the base case, ADDITIONAL power demand will be 70GW.

- Elon Musk sees 1 billion humanoid robots by 2040.

Where's the power coming from?

Where will the data centers be located?

What about cooling water?

Infrastructure?

I prefer to invest in these bottlenecks instead of picking the next winner in the robot race.

Power demand is rising so fast that The Wall Street Journal asked, "Who Pays? AI Boom Sparks Fight Over Soaring Power Costs," in the title on July 29.

It noted that power demand is so high that utilities will soon be spending more than $230 billion per year on infrastructure, a blowout number that's twice the number they were spending before the pandemic.

Because of rapid growth, networks have simply hit their limits, which has led to costs nobody wants to bear, especially not the data center operators, who could be faced with substantial margin compression. Meanwhile, households won't be able to take on these costs either, as inflation is already an issue.

Google, Amazon, Microsoft and other data-center developers earlier this month pushed back on Dominion’s proposals, saying they go too far in shifting risk onto the companies. Each said the measures would drive up the cost of development and slow growth in a market where it already takes years for data centers to connect to the grid. - WSJ

As dramatic as this may sound, I believe the rapid data center expansion near populated areas is over. It's simply not possible to continue at this pace due to water, power, and infrastructure constraints.

After all, we haven't even seen the next stage of AI data center expansions.

For now, it was mostly demand to service entry-level services.

Natural Gas is Where It's at

Nuclear power is fantastic. It's cheap, reliable, non-polluting, and more dense than any other energy source known to mankind.

However, we're still decades away from mass adoption for reasons tied to regulations, innovation, labor shortages, securing uranium supply, and more.

For now, natural gas remains the key.

  • Natural gas is super cheap in the United States. It's even cheaper in areas like the Permian Basin, where booming natural gas output has created takeaway/egress capacity issues, dropping the price of Waha natural gas to almost zero.
  • Natural gas is much cleaner than coal.
  • Natural gas is one of the most reliable sources of energy. Bear in mind, data centers need reliable energy, which limits the use of wind and solar power.
  • North America has so much natural gas, one could almost say it's "unlimited."

This creates opportunities.

For example, in Texas, data centers are being built right on the pipelines, creating a "behind the meter" system where the strain on the public power network is minimal.

Traditional grid interconnections simply can’t keep pace with hyperscalers’ power demands, especially as AI accelerates energy requirements,” Sailfish founder Ryan Hughes told the website Data Center Dynamics at the time. “Our on-site natural gas power islands will let customers scale quickly.”

CloudBurst and Energy Transfer announced their data center and power plant outside New Braunfels in February, and another company partnership also announced plans for a 250 megawatt gas plant and data center near Odessa in West Texas. In May, a developer called Tract announced a 1,500-acre, 2,000 megawatt data center campus with some on-site generation and some purchased gas power near the small Central Texas town of Lockhart. - Texas Tribune

This is very bullish for midstream companies like Energy Transfer (ET), which is a Master Limited Partnership, Kinder Morgan (KMI), which is a natural gas-focused C-Corp, and TC Energy (TRP), a Canadian pure-play natural gas pipeline giant that expects extremely high demand for pipelines.

Between 2024 and 2035, it sees a natural gas demand surge of 45 billion cubic feet per day to nearly 170 billion cubic feet per day. That's "wild."

I believe all of these midstream companies have >5% yields and are terrific investments for conservative investors as they do not have direct pricing exposure due to their fixed-fee contract structures with natural gas producers.

However, for investors willing to take on more risk, I think there are four companies the market is still sleeping on. These companies are Tourmaline Oil (TOU:CA), ARC Resources (ARX:CA), Antero Resources (AR), and Range Resources (RRC), four companies I have increasingly covered on this website.

While I like most major natural gas companies in North America (this is key for me to mention!), these four companies all bring something special to the table, which is a mix of super deep reserves and ultra low-cost operations.

In other words, these companies have more natural gas than nearly anyone else and some of the best drilling reserves, which allows them to stay profitable when others are forced to cut production and to become cash gushers when natural gas prices rise.

See, while Henry Hub natural gas prices are very low at around $3 and a total pain to monitor daily, I believe in a sustained long-term uptrend. This is driven by declining production growth (see below), increasing LNG exports, and a general alignment with international prices, which are much higher.

Using EIA data (see below), we see that international prices are up to four times higher, which means even a small convergence over time triggered by more exports could cause Henry Hub to trade at substantially higher prices.

Natural gas futures for delivery at the Title Transfer Facility (TTF) in the Netherlands decreased 12 cents to a weekly average of $11.32/MMBtu. In the same week last year (week ending July 31, 2024), the prices were $12.35/MMBtu in East Asia and $10.70/MMBtu at TTF. - EIA

From a pricing point of view, I truly believe these companies will be able to double their revenues in the years ahead, which bodes very well for shareholders.

ARC Resources, for example, offers a 2.8% yield and has a substantial presence in the Western Canadian Sedimentary Basin, where its peer Tourmaline also operates. It aims for at least 10% annual dividend growth regardless of natural gas prices, with an ultra-low payout ratio of just 15%. Most of its cash is used for buybacks, which I believe will transform the company into a high-growth total return stock.

By 2028, the company's production profile will allow for more than C$4.20 in free cash flow per share, roughly 15% of its market cap, a number that hints at elevated, double-digit annual shareholder returns.

Meanwhile, Tourmaline, which yields 3.5% (base yield), pays both a base and a special dividend, with an expected free cash flow per share boost to C$7.60 by 2031, fueled by higher output, better access to LNG exports, and structural pricing tailwinds. This gives it a free cash flow yield similar to ARC Resources.

Regarding its dividend, the combination of quarterly special dividends and its base dividend results in a current total yield of 6.0%, one of the best numbers in the entire industry.

When it comes to hidden data center winners and general energy gems, I think it's nearly impossible to beat the value these two bring to the table.

For dividends, US natural gas companies Antero Resources and Range Resources are useless, as Range pays 1.0%, while Antero pays no dividend.

However, that's OK, as both have massive total return potential.

For example, at just $4.50, Range Resources generates roughly $1.2 billion in free cash flow, 14% of its market cap. As I expect Henry Hub prices to average at least $5-$6 in the years ahead (conservatively speaking), I believe this could become one of the most aggressive buyback companies in the U.S.

Even better, at $5, the total PV-10 value of its proven reserves is roughly $68 per share, nearly twice its total stock price. Even at current prices, investors are just paying 1x the PV-10 value for its existing reserves.

These numbers are similar for Antero Resources, which has outperformed its three peers by a huge margin over the past five years, mainly supported by its poor performance before and during the pandemic.

While natural gas prices remain challenging and super volatile, I believe these are some of the most hidden opportunities that come with tremendous free cash flow opportunities, dividend potential, and what I expect to be outperforming capital gains for at least a full decade.

However, please be aware that these are very volatile investments. Take this into account when doing your due diligence.

Also, to answer the one big question I expect to pop up in the comments, I do not own any of these stocks due to my investments in Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ), which is an oil and gas giant. I also have more than a quarter of my entire portfolio in LandBridge (LB) and Texas Pacific Land (TPL), which are Permian Basin landowners with exposure to oil and gas royalties, water royalties, and surface operations like data centers.

If I had more capital and a less concentrated portfolio, I would be a buyer of either ARC Resources or Tourmaline, as I like that they pay a dividend on top of their buyback potential.

Who knows, maybe I'll buy one of the two next year. I would love to.

Takeaway

After everything we discussed in this article, I think it's fair to say the AI revolution isn't just another tech wave. I believe it's a paradigm shift we're still struggling to understand.

Our "failure of imagination" keeps us from fully understanding its potential scale over the next few years, which includes data centers consuming power like entire nations, humanoids reshaping labor, and energy demand that overwhelms our infrastructure.

For investors like you and me, this isn't just about picking AI winners. I'm convinced it's about betting on the backbone of this revolution.

That's where natural gas comes in.

With its reliability and abundance, it could be the biggest bottleneck of the AI revolution.

While midstream giants like Energy Transfer and Kinder Morgan offer stability, the real upside lies in low-cost producers like Tourmaline Oil, ARC Resources, and their American peers, Range and Antero Resources, which are poised to thrive in a higher-price (and demand) environment.

Essentially, if you're willing to look beyond the risks of elevated volatility, the energy behind AI might be the decade's most overlooked opportunity.

I truly believe natural gas has become a solution to the bottleneck of one of the biggest technological transitions since the first Industrial Revolution.

Risks to My Thesis

The biggest risk is cyclical weakness, which could (as it's currently doing) hurt natural gas prices and energy stocks, in general. On a longer-term basis, the major risk comes from energy alternatives like nuclear power. However, I do not expect this to turn into a realistic alternative until at least the late 2030s.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: IVW: A Balanced Alternative To Growth
data.description:
Based on a disciplined investment strategy, the iShares S&P 500 Growth ETF (IVW) is among the alternatives within the growth investment theme. Although the fund maintains a strong bias toward the tech sector, it has a more balanced exposure across the equity market, which allows it to trade at lower valuations and exhibit relatively more contained volatility compared to more aggressive peers. It is a middle-ground approach that may help investors navigate the current environment.

ETF Description & Highlights

IVW is an ETF managed by BlackRock, providing exposure to U.S. large companies with growth characteristics. It tracks the S&P 500 Growth Index that follows the S&P Dow Jones Indices methodology.

This index selects companies from the S&P 500 based on three key measures: earnings growth over the past three years, sales growth over the past three years, and trailing twelve-month price change. The index is structured based on a segmentation of the S&P 500 market cap into three parts, each totaling one-third of the index's market cap: 1) growth style (according to the three measures just listed), 2) value style (according to measures like book to price, earnings to price, and sales to price), and 3) blended style (defined as those which exhibit similar growth and value characteristics)

As a next step, companies classified as growth styles are weighted according to their float market cap. In addition, those companies with blended styles are also weighted based on the float market cap, but with a twist: they are subject to a cap to adjust their weights based on the strength of the growth style relative to the value style. While it may seem like a complex calculator, it serves a clear purpose of maximizing the participation of growth-style companies in the index, while those with blended styles have a more moderate share in the index. The index is also subject to quarterly rebalances and caps, limiting individual stock weights to 23% and the combined stocks weighing more than 4.8% to 50% to avoid concentration.

As of July 29, 2025, IVW managed $61.3 billion in assets across 212 holdings, with an average market cap of $699 billion. The fund has a strong bias toward megacaps, which make up 65.7% versus 46.9% in the S&P 500 index, represented by the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). Another 24.3% is allocated to large caps, leaving only 9.8% to midcaps, while the S&P 500 has 17.9% in this category.

IVW is heavily skewed toward tech stocks. Four out of its ten top holdings are from the technology sector: NVIDIA (15.01%), Microsoft (6.81%), Apple (4.93%), Broadcom (4.89%), and Amazon (4.33%). Other top holdings include Meta (5.18%), Alphabet A and C shares (7.11% combined), Tesla (3.07%), and Visa (2.06%), which together account for 53% of the total fund, signaling a focused exposure to top holdings, exacerbating a somewhat existing trend in the S&P 500, where the top ten holdings represent 38% of the index.

The following table presents a list of six growth-oriented ETFs that will serve as the peer group for IVW in this analysis. The two largest funds are Vanguard Growth ETF (VUG) and iShares Russell 1000 Growth ETF (IWF), both passively tracking broad-based large-cap growth indexes, and with a strong allocation to megacaps as well. Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG) mirrors a similar strategy by tracking the Dow Jones U.S. Large-Cap Growth Index, while Vanguard Mega Cap Growth ETF (MGK) gives emphasis on mega caps (73%), following the CRSP Mega Cap Growth Index. Meanwhile, the iShares Core S&P U.S. Growth ETF (IUSG) has a slightly more diversified exposure with over 400 holdings. Lastly, Nuveen Growth Opportunities ETF (NUGO) is the only actively managed fund in the group, relying on a concentrated portfolio of mega-cap names.

IVW leans heavily into the technology sector as a whole, which represents 43.9% of the fund. The second-largest sector is communication services, comprising 14.6%, followed by consumer discretionary at 11.9%, and financial services at 10.9%. The industrials sector also has a meaningful allocation of 7.4% and healthcare accounts for 5.1%. Minor allocations include consumer staples (3.2%), utilities (1.2%), real estate (1.1%), basic materials (0.4%), and energy (0.4%). Compared to the S&P 500, IVW is overweight in technology (+8.6%) and communication services (+4.7%), as expected, while it underweights healthcare (-3.7%), financial services (-2.5%), and energy (-2.6%), sectors that generally have more mature operations.

The fund's allocation to technology is marked by large positions in semiconductors and software. While Microsoft remains a core position, what draws attention is its overweight holdings in NVIDIA, Broadcom, Oracle, and Palantir, along with nearly 30 smaller positions in the two segments. Yet, Apple is still a key holding with nearly 5%, despite the fund's underweight allocation to the iPhone maker.

Consumer discretionary is another sector where IVW shows a concentrated allocation. Key holdings are Amazon and Tesla. Beyond these two megacaps, the fund takes small but diversified stakes in entertainment (DoorDash, Booking Holdings, and Royal Caribbean), home improvement (Home Depot), and auto retail (O'Reilly Automotive).

Financial services is the fund's third-largest sector, where consumer finance is by far the core industry with overweight holdings in Visa, Mastercard, and American Express. Another core allocation is to JPMorgan, while other marginal positions are spread across segments like capital markets, insurance, and brokerage.

Outside these largest sectors, IVW's allocation includes fairly large positions in Meta Platforms and Alphabet. Beyond that, positions in pharmaceuticals (Eli Lilly and AbbVie), hypermarkets (Costco and Walmart), and industrials (RTX and Caterpillar) provide some degree of diversification to the fund.

Technology is clearly a go-to sector for most growth-oriented funds. Despite its large stance in the sector, IVW underweights the sector (-7.4%) relative to peers, as all funds, with the exception of IUSG, allocate more than 50% to the sector. Meanwhile, IVW overweights financial services (+4.0%) and industrials (+2.5%), as most peers take a conservative view on these sectors, despite the cyclical nature of industrials, for instance.

But these allocation gaps should be considered a minor dislocation, broadly speaking. IVW shows a strong sector correlation of over 97% with all funds, which makes growth ETFs one of the most aligned categories in the equity market. At the individual stock level, the correlation is also strong in general, with funds like IWF, SCHG, and IUSG exhibiting portfolio overlaps of over 70%.

Valuation Above The S&P 500, But Still Lower Than Peers

At a P/E of 29.6x, IVW trades at a 25.5% premium to the S&P 500. This primarily reflects the fund's overweight allocation to technology and communications services, where average valuations are higher than the benchmark. A second key factor stems from IVW's richer valuation across most sectors. This is particularly significant in consumer discretionary, given the fund's large exposure to Tesla and Amazon, but also in consumer staples, as core holdings like Costco and Walmart trade above 30x P/E.

IVW delivers on growth. Its historical earnings growth (23.1%) is more than twice the S&P 500 (9.2%), driven by stronger growth in technology and financial services. Likewise, IVW also has an edge on profitability. Its EBITDA margin of 39.2% is 2.6 percentage points higher than the S&P 500's 35.6%, while its ROE of 57.1% is handily above the index. This reflects large margins in technology driving the fund's margins up, while we can also see a marginally higher profitability profile in key sectors like technology and financial services, with key contributions from NVIDIA, Broadcom, Visa, Mastercard, and Meta Platforms.

While IVW's P/E is high compared to the S&P 500, valuations in the peer group of growth ETFs are even higher. Funds like VUG, IWF, and SCHG trade in the range of 31x to 32x P/E, driven by their larger positions in technology and consumer discretionary. This scenario is exacerbated in MGK and NUGO due to their heavier positions in mega caps. As a result, their valuations surpass 33x P/E, which makes IVW a reasonable option for investors cautious about high multiples in these growth-oriented funds.

Strong Returns, But Lagging Other Growth Funds

IVW's gains over the past few years have been much stronger than the S&P 500, as evidenced by its annualized returns of 19.0% over the 3-year window versus the S&P 500's 16.5%. But the fund has also been more volatile during corrections. In 2022, it underperformed the benchmark by 11.4%. This caused IVW's annualized returns over the past five years to be just a touch higher than the S&P 500 (15.8% vs 15.4%). Meanwhile, IVW has slightly lagged behind peers over time, in a performance that has seen some unusual weak correlations, like in 2020 and 2022, which compromised its overall performance on a relative basis.

Despite that, IVW has seen relatively more contained volatility than peers over the past years, with beta in the range of 1.05 to 1.12, a few percentage points lower than all peers. This is likely driven by peers' heavier exposure to technology and consumer discretionary, two high-beta sectors by nature. However, IVW's volatility remains higher than the S&P 500, as expected for a growth fund, where we can see the fund and peers sharing a common historical tendency to experience wider price action both in upside and downside, as measured by their capture ratios of over 1.02 in both directions.

IVW has a heavy average daily volume of $233 million, leading to a narrow spread of 0.01%, as low as the most liquid peers. Despite its 31% turnover, the highest within the group, its expense ratio sits at a reasonable 0.18%, which is in line with IWF, but higher than most cost-effective peers like VUG and SCHG. IVW offers a low TTM yield of 0.44%, similar to peers, as their growth tilt results in low exposure to higher-yielding sectors, like financial services, consumer staples, and healthcare. Lastly, its tracking error at nearly 6% indicates a stable correlation with the underlying index, while IVW's Sharpe ratio of 0.87 (3-year) and 0.73 (5-year) are fairly high. Despite that, it has trailed all peers, driven by their stronger returns over time, as the overall bullish environment over the past year has favored their more aggressive strategies.

Final Thoughts

Based on a balanced approach toward growth stocks, IVW maintains a clear orientation toward larger companies, particularly big techs like NVIDIA, Microsoft, and Meta Platforms, yet without the overwhelming concentration seen in other growth funds. As a result, valuations sit in the middle ground, still elevated versus the S&P 500 but lower than most peers. This positioning adds a layer of stability to the portfolio, with more contained volatility than other growth alternatives. The flip side is that IVW has lagged more aggressive growth funds in an overall bullish market over the past years.

Looking ahead, while corporate earnings have continued to support the equity market, the path forward is less clear in the near term, given the prospect of slower growth amid arguably lofty valuations. With that in mind, although IVW may not offer the highest upside in risk-on environments, it remains a measured alternative for investors looking to maintain growth exposure without leaning too heavily into the tech-driven space as most aggressive alternatives do.

data.title: Arteris: Unlocking Long-Term Value In The AI Semiconductor Ecosystem
data.description:
Introduction: The Undervalued Architect of AI's Future

In the wake of the 'revolutionary' AI chipmakers that scoop headlines and dominate the market, the investment community frequently undervalues enabling technologies - the technology that makes the advanced semiconductor technologies a reality. One of these enabling technology companies is Arteris, a key but often ignored player in the AI revolution. Arteris designs and licenses the interconnect "fabric" IP that connects multiple components in a system-on-chip and enables complex communication between them, a critical component for every modern AI, automotive, or data center processor.

My primary investment thesis is that the market is massively undervaluing Arteris (NASDAQ:AIP).

Investors seem focused on its current lack of GAAP profitability, failing to price in two key factors: the immense long-term value of its compounding royalty revenue model and its recent, pivotal endorsement from AMD. The market neglect of Arteris has also created an extraordinary opportunity for long-term investors to establish long-term positions in Arteris today. After conducting a thorough analysis of Arteris' financial situation, identifying growth catalysts, and using a conservative peer-based valuation, I am rating Arteris as a Strong Buy with a price target of $23.00 per share.

Company Background

Picture a state-of-the-art, ultra-complicated microchip as a city. The city has various neighborhoods, a central processing unit [CPU] as city hall, a graphics processing unit [GPU] as the art district, artificial intelligence processors (NPUs) as the advanced thinking district, and memory banks as the library in our city. For this city to function, it must have a complicated highway system to travel billions of pieces of information instantly between neighborhoods without traffic. Arteris builds that digital highway system.

Arteris does not manufacture the chips. Rather, as described in its May 2025 Corporate Overview, Arteris designs and licenses all the essential plans (the Intellectual Property) to build an on-chip network, or Network-on-Chip (NoC). Now, as chips for AI and autonomous driving become exponentially more complex with hundreds of processing engines on a piece of silicon, a highly efficient and intelligent "traffic control" system is no longer a nice-to-have; it has become an absolute need. Arteris's technology helps get data to where it needs to go with minimal time and energy consumption, which is essential for the next generation of electronics to perform.

The business model is very powerful and scoped for long-term compounding growth. Arteris is a two-phase business.

License Fees: A customer, such as a major semiconductor company, pays Arteris a one-time upfront fee to get authorized to use Arteris's NoC design tools & plans. This initial payment generates the first revenue stream and covers the cost of development and professional services.

Royalties: This is the really valuable part of the business. When the customer integrates Arteris's IP into their final chip design and goes to mass production, the customer pays Arteris a small fee for every single unit they release into the stream of commerce. Whether a chip goes into a new car, data center server, or smartphone, Arteris gets their cut. This creates a long-tail, high-margin, recurring revenue stream that could last for many years, long after the initial design work is concluded.

Arteris is the dominant player in its space, especially in the demanding automotive and AI spaces. The company's technology is already included in billions of chips globally. Arteris is a foundational technology partner, often invisible, to the world's most prominent technology companies.

Financials

A fair review of Arteris's financial condition reflects a classic investment-for-growth story where Arteris has a strong balance sheet and attractive legal ownership business model but is producing large operating losses, amidst investments in expansion. The strength of this balance sheet is central to the investment thesis. It provides a financial cushion that allows Arteris to absorb near-term losses from its strategic investments.

Balance Sheet

Based on Arteris's Q1 2025 balance sheet, the company has substantial cash and equivalents on its balance sheet and near-zero long-term debt. This clean balance sheet provides the company substantial operating flexibility and protects it from any interest rate variability. Additionally, it provides a runway for the company to fund its operations and strategic investments without going to the capital markets to fund operations and further dilute shareholder value. The financial backdrop is a very effective de-risking component that allows the company to focus its efforts on tech development and seizing market share.

Income Statement

A look at the income statement tells two stories. The top-line story tells the story of the attractiveness of the IP licensing model. Qualitative advantages associated with legal ownership of an IP model have permitted the company to produce gross margins just above 90%, as disclosed in the notes to the Q2 Earnings Report. Revenues comprise up-front license fees from the license fees typically disclosed under revenue recognition, and ongoing royalties. Revenues have primarily been from license fees, and we are in the formal transition to the long-tail royalty business and continue to be in the early innings of the licensing game.

Above the gross profit line, all is clear sailing, but below the gross profit line tells a different story. The company has not historically been GAAP profitable due to high and deliberate operating costs (intentional, as explained in earlier notes). The largest form of expense is the R&D expense, which is of not inconsequential size relative to total revenues. This statement is not a reflection of inefficiency; instead, it is a statement of strategy. The company is more historically investing today to create the next-generation IP that is being called for deals such as AMD that was just announced, with design wins being the first seeds towards the royalty payments streaming.

Sales and marketing expenses are also large with the company simply trying to grow the number of customers. Netted out, we have a glaring GAAP net loss. This net GAAP loss is the biggest "bad" on the minds of investors, but it is important to note that the total GAAP loss has to be viewed in the context of the "good" it is driving, which is a favorable optimal market position for the company, and a new and potentially large upstream revenue stream that will be flowing from royalty/usage based revenue streams.

Cash Flow

The cash flow statement is the most straightforward picture of the financial position, and it makes it very clear this company is burning cash. The cash burn from operations is the incremental cash cost of the growth strategy presented in the income statement. While the cash burn is a risk, it appears manageable when compared to the cash on the statement of financial position. The main takeaway is to have investors benchmark burn broadly to forward-looking relevant benchmarks such as the company's Annual Recurring Revenue [ARR] and Remaining Performance Obligation [RPO] which were indicated to be growing in the earnings call. Provided the investment (cash burn) can ultimately build a larger future revenue RPO backlog, then the plan is working. The risk is that cash burn continues until some future date, growth picks up, and cash flow positivity results for the company.

In summary, Arteris is presenting finances as a growth company and not as profit generating mature company. The company is attempting, as much as it can, to utilize a strong balance sheet and absorb short-term losses today to invest across the organization in R&D, build, and build the foundations in the AI semiconductor market.

Once accomplished, if all goes to plan, AIP can create extremely profitable and predictable royalty revenue going forward.

5 August 2025 Q2 Earnings Update

Follow up to our deep dive into Arteris, the recently released Q2 2025 earnings results from the company, and their recent investor presentation provide some important new color and even affirm the long-term thesis we spun out.

Arteris reported quarterly revenue of $16.5 million, up 13% year-over-year, to go along with notable new high-water marks for both annual contract value + royalties ($69.1 million) and remaining performance obligations ($99.3 million). These metrics clearly demonstrate the Arteris pipeline is on the ascent, especially in the A.I. and automotive verticals which we pointed out as the key catalytic growth drivers of the Arteris business model. While Arteris still loses money on a GAAP basis reporting a net loss of $7.8 million in the quarter, the healthy above average gross margins and mildly declining non-GAAP operating loss figures only support the viability of the high value low-cost IP licensing model we pitched. Financially the cash balances are solid, there is no material debt, and the company is well positioned to continue growth without meaningful dilution to shareholders.

The most recent investor presentation also pointed to several new developments which further strengthen the case for Arteris as a foundational company in the semiconductor ecosystem.

FlexGen Smart NoC IP roll out should provide meaningful contributions on the improved engineering productivity levels but also improved power consumption levels, while also providing Arteris some credibility as chip complexity accelerates. As we have seen with the new design wins with Infineon, GigaDevice, and Menta while continuing to deepen relationships with existing top-tier customers, Arteris is not just continuing to make inroads into the semiconductor ecosystem, but instead is becoming entrenched with the hottest next-generation chip designs. Momentum in the A.I. and automotive domains, aided by the recent AMD partnership, is indeed resulting in actual traction in the business.

That being said, Arteris stock was down more than 10% based on after-hours trading compared to where it was priced before the results were released. It appears that the stock move was a combination of disappointing full-year guidance, what will clearly be ongoing cash burn, and a regular 'sell the news' event after the recent rise in share price. Some investors have become impatient with the timing of profitability, despite the fundamentals being intact. The company continues to execute on the plans shared, the backlog is increasing in value, and the company is ramped up to execute on the long-term royalty compounding story we shared. Moreover, I believe the most recent results and business developments only better back up the view that Arteris is building a premium, high margin royalty-driven business at the heart of the AI and automotive chip revolution. For long-term investors, just think of the volatility as an opportunity, not a red flag. The progress being made by the company across all the financial, technological, and strategic initiatives demonstrated in the investor presentation suggests that the marketplace will ultimately be forced to understand the fundamental value of the company, especially when we start to see the compounding effects of its royalty-driven model.

Valuation

For Arteris valuation, I've used a Comparable Company Analysis using a forward Enterprise Value-to-sales (EV/sales) multiple, as this is the most appropriate methodology for a high growth, not-yet-profitable company in the software/IP domain. A DCF, would be much less reliable at this point, given the challenges in estimating the exact timing of the royalty streams, and the timing of Arteris path to positive free cash flow.

I'm assuming the following key concepts as a foundation for my valuation:

Revenue Growth: Given the strong pipeline comments and significant impact of successful AI design wins, I believe a forward revenue estimate of $75 million (next 12 months) is reasonable. This estimate is an extension of company guidance of $66-$70 million for full year 2025, which implies an approximate 30% bottom line growth from the previous period. I think that the ramp is warranted given the momentum gained from new AI partnerships, particularly with AMD, and a record high Remaining Performance Obligations (RPO) backlog that indicate revenue will be ramping up as these significant contracts have a greater impact over the course of the following quarters.

Peer Group: The two premier semiconductor IP and EDA (Electronic Design Automation) categories of players are Synopsys (SNPS) and Cadence Design Systems (CDNS), which are large, profitable companies trading at the premium forward EV/Sales multiples from Finviz of approximately 18 (Synopsys) and 19 (Cadence); the market is giving these categories the premium multiples due to the mission-critical software and sticky customer relationship they have developed.

Valuation Multiple: Arteris, is smaller and does not have positive profits, so we are going to need to provide a discount to the larger industry leaders, but arguably has better growth rates. I will use a multiple of 11x of forward EV/sales, which is a discount to much larger peers and reflects the strong growth characteristics of the company.

The mathematics of calculation is:

Projected Forward Revenue: $75 million

Applied/Valuation EV/Sales Multiple: 11.0x

Calculated Enterprise Value [EV]: $825 million

To arrive at the fair value market cap, I will need to adjust for the net cash position of the company . Based on the last balance sheet of Arteris, the company has approximately $50 million in cash and little debt.

Enterprise Value: $825 million

Add: Cash-net cash: $50 million

Calculated Fair Value Market Cap: $875 million

In the current environment, Arteris has about 38 million shares outstanding, yielding a price target of approximately:

Target Price = $875 million market cap / 38 million shares = ~$23.00/share

The price target represents an upside of more than 100% relative to current valuations and points to the significant disconnect between Arteris current price in the market and its value.

Key Investment Risks

The main risks to my thesis are the following:

Competition: Arteris competes against large semiconductor companies that all have their own internal IP divisions, as well as the established EDA players such as Synopsys and Cadence, who can leverage their scale to compete.

Customer Product Delays: Arteris's royalty revenue is directly tied to the success of its customers with getting their chips to market and ramp to volume. Hence, substantial product delay from a major product like the AMD AI chiplet would delay this highly profitable source of revenue.

Timeline to Profitability: The company is in a strong financial position, however, any failure to become cash flow positive within the next 2-3 years could impact the stock and potentially require additional sources of capital.

Final Takeaway

With Arteris, we have a rare chance to invest in the core layer of the AI boom at a good value. We now have confirmation that Arteris is at the start of a sustained long-term bull market, because they are executing with the right, best-in-class technology, in a partnership with a world-class leader like AMD, and they are growing in a hyper-scalable, high margin business model direction (which means the market's short-term belief in profitability, is missing the compounding of Arteris's future royalty stream).

We stood in the same hinge moment, as now, and this is being further confirmed by the stock price technicals. The news of their partnership with AMD was huge, and the stock exploded by over +30% right away and put the company squarely on the map of a much bigger universe of further investors. This was not a "one and done" move, there was deep conviction behind this move, as evidenced in the trading volume on August 5th, when shares traded over 15x of their average volume, the highest volume ever since the IPO. This type of price action and volume is usually one of several classic signs, from a technical take, that a fundamental shift has occurred in investor thinking, and this will be the start (go signal) of the deteriorating move (multi-weeks/multi-months rally) up. That reported level is 12.40, recent highs at the end of 2024 are now supported.

A pullback on lower volume in the next few days would be great to find a low-risk entry for people who were not able to enter on the first day of this big move.

At the end of the day, the fundamental story, push-pull strategy events, and its emerging technical revenue style momentum are all aligned for a bright future. I am putting a strong "Buy" rating on the stock, with a target price $23.00.

data.title: CommScope's Leaner Strategy Supports A Cautious Buy After Major Price Surge
data.description:
About a week and a half ago, I rated CommScope Holding Company, Inc. (COMM) a buy at $8. Since then, the stock has climbed to $15.65 before settling just under $15 as of August 6, 2025. That’s an 85 percent climb in two weeks and a 550 percent gain over the past year. With numbers like these, it’s only fair to ask whether this rapid rise still has fuel, or if caution is now the smarter move.

What’s Driving This Wild Ride?

This surge isn’t just luck or market noise. CommScope has made some bold moves, including the sale of its Connectivity and Cable Solutions Segment to Amphenol for $10.5 billion. This was a massive part of their operations and included several products tied to fast-growing markets like AI. By letting it go, CommScope is now focusing more on its network access and cellular solutions units, which management believes can drive growth and fatter profit margins. The latest press releases have made it clear that CommScope is doubling down on a strategy that puts profits and focus ahead of just size.

The Company Today: Sharper Focus, Smaller Footprint

After the big sale, CommScope looks a lot leaner and more focused. They are no longer spreading themselves thin across too many business lines. Instead, they’re putting their chips on areas that can grow faster and deliver higher returns. Q2 2025 results tell the story. Sales jumped by 32 percent year-over-year to $1.39 billion.

Gross margins rose to 42.6 percent, showing better efficiency and smarter execution. Operating income almost tripled compared to the same time last year. Sure, net income was boosted by a one-off gain but even looking past that, the trend is clear. The company cut its debt by over $2 billion in just a few months, now sitting at $7.25 billion and cash on hand has climbed to $571 million. Yes, operating cash flow is still in the red but a lot of that comes from swings in working capital, not a deeper problem. Management stands by its full-year EBITDA goal of $1.15 to $1.2 billion, which is a sign they believe the new CommScope can keep up the momentum.

A Fast-Moving Industry and CommScope’s Place in It

The demand for stronger, more reliable networks is only getting bigger, thanks to AI, the cloud and all things 5G. CommScope finds itself right in the middle, with both wired and wireless offerings and deep relationships with the big telecom and cable players. If the company can hold on to its customer base and keep rolling out new, useful products, there’s a real shot to gain even more ground. News headlines point to fresh strategic partnerships and plenty of investor excitement after the asset sale and the recent earnings beat.

What the Numbers Say About Value

With the stock at $14.77, CommScope now trades at about 11 times expected earnings for 2025 and an enterprise value to EBITDA ratio of around 11. That’s no super cheap price but it isn’t too high given the strong growth and cleaner strategy. The key point is this: as the sale money comes in and goes toward paying off more debt, CommScope’s enterprise value, which adds market cap and debt together before subtracting cash, will drop. The stock will look cheaper compared to profits, even if earnings themselves do not move much right away.

If management can deliver on its goals, keep margins strong and continue to pay down debt as expected, the EV/EBITDA multiple should fall over time. The company will also have lower interest costs and more flexibility to use its cash for growth projects, stock buybacks, or even future dividends. Because of this, the recent run in the share price is about more than just excitement, it reflects real, positive changes to CommScope’s financial health and future outlook.

When you put it all together, the asset sale lets CommScope put its resources into faster-growing business lines and it also makes the current stock price look more attractive once the impact on its financial structure is fully realized. If CommScope keeps meeting its targets, it is not hard to imagine shares moving into the $16 to $18 range over the next year, which would give investors a respectable upside from where things are now.

What Could Get in the Way

Of course, not everything is smooth sailing. Debt remains high, even after the recent reduction and interest payments will still take a bite out of cash flow if things go sideways. The company is now more concentrated, which means any mistake, whether it’s losing a customer, a failed new product, or a slowdown in the industry, could hit harder than before. CommScope is also less tied to AI and the hottest areas of tech after selling the Connectivity and Cable Solutions unit. If those sectors really explode, the company may be left watching from the sidelines.

Looking Ahead: What I’m Watching Closely

The next few quarters will be key. I want to see CommScope make good on its new business plan, keep lifting margins and avoid any big surprises with cash flow or customer contracts. Analyst estimates are generally positive and management talks a good game but what happens next on the ground will matter more than any guidance or projection.

How the Future Might Play Out

If things go wrong, maybe the smaller CommScope stumbles and can’t keep up the pace, which could mean the stock gives up some of its gains, especially if the broader market turns rocky. The most likely scenario, in my mind, is that the company hits its goals, keeps growing profit, and the stock moves higher but at a slower, steadier pace. If everything works out, CommScope might land new deals or grab back some AI and data center work, and that could open the door to even higher prices.

Wrapping It Up: Still A Buy but Be Thoughtful

So where do I land? I’m sticking with a buy, but with a much more careful approach than before. For new investors, I’d wait for dips closer to $13 or $14 to start a position. The company’s outlook is brighter, but there’s less room for mistakes. If management delivers, there’s still value here. This isn’t a high-risk bet anymore, but for anyone who wants a stake in a sharper, faster CommScope and is willing to keep a close watch, it deserves a spot on your radar.
